request_debug.prompt_preview:
You rerank dead-startup candidates for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and K candidates retrieved by hybrid search, score each candidate from four perspectives, then return ONLY the top 5 ranked from most to least relevant.

Perspectives (each scored 0-10 with a one-sentence rationale):
- business_model: how similar are the revenue, pricing, and customer-relationship shapes?
- market: how similar are the target buyers, market size, and demand drivers?
- gtm: how similar are the go-to-

response.text: {"ranked":[{"candidate_id":"ubiome::biotech","perspective_scores":{"business_model":{"score":5,"rationale":"uBiome sold direct-to-consumer health kits with a recurring/subscription element, sharing the B2C consumer health monetization shape, though it was kit-based rather than a monthly app subscription."},"market":{"score":7,"rationale":"Both target health-conscious consumers seeking personal health insights, though uBiome focused on microbiome curiosity rather than chronic-illness management specifically."},"gtm":{"score":6,"rationale":"Both used direct-to-consumer digital channels to acquire individual health consumers without a traditional clinical sales force."},"stage_scale":{"score":5,"rationale":"uBiome was a venture-backed startup that failed at a growth stage with hundreds of thousands of customers, roughly comparable scale to an early-stage consumer healthtech app."}},"rationale":"uBiome is the closest match as a direct-to-consumer health startup selling personal health data services, sharing B2C health focus and digital GTM, though its product was physical kits and it failed due to fraud rather than product-market fit issues."},{"candidate_id":"theranos::healthtech","perspective_scores":{"business_model":{"score":3,"rationale":"Theranos operated a B2B2C diagnostic testing model through retail partnerships rather than a direct monthly subscription app, making the revenue shape quite different."},"market":{"score":6,"rationale":"Both target everyday consumers seeking accessible healthcare solutions, and Theranos positioned itself around making health testing convenient and accessible to the general public."},"gtm":{"score":3,"rationale":"Theranos relied on retail pharmacy partnerships (Walgreens, Safeway) rather than a mobile-first direct subscription GTM motion."},"stage_scale":{"score":3,"rationale":"Theranos reached a $9 billion valuation and hundreds of locations before collapse, far larger scale than an early-stage consumer app."}},"rationale":"Theranos shares the healthtech sector and consumer-accessibility narrative but differs substantially in business model, GTM, and scale, making it a weak but second-best comparable among the candidates."},{"candidate_id":"jawbone::hardware","perspective_scores":{"business_model":{"score":3,"rationale":"Jawbone sold hardware devices (fitness trackers, headsets) at one-time retail price points rather than recurring subscription bundles, a fundamentally different revenue shape."},"market":{"score":5,"rationale":"Jawbone's UP fitness trackers targeted health-conscious consumers tracking personal wellness metrics, overlapping somewhat with chronic-illness self-management audiences."},"gtm":{"score":3,"rationale":"Jawbone sold through retail and hardware channels rather than app stores or direct digital subscription funnels."},"stage_scale":{"score":4,"rationale":"Jawbone raised over $900 million and was a large-scale consumer hardware company, significantly larger than a typical early-stage subscription app."}},"rationale":"Jawbone shares the consumer health tracking angle but is primarily a hardware company with one-time purchase revenue and retail distribution, making it a distant analogue."},{"candidate_id":"pebble::hardware","perspective_scores":{"business_model":{"score":2,"rationale":"Pebble sold hardware smartwatches through crowdfunding and retail, with no recurring subscription or telehealth component."},"market":{"score":3,"rationale":"Pebble targeted tech-savvy early adopters rather than chronic-illness patients, with only superficial overlap in wearable health-adjacent positioning."},"gtm":{"score":3,"rationale":"Pebble used crowdfunding and retail hardware distribution, entirely different from a mobile app subscription GTM."},"stage_scale":{"score":5,"rationale":"Pebble was a venture-backed startup that failed before achieving mass scale, roughly analogous in stage to an early consumer app."}},"rationale":"Pebble is a consumer mobile-companion hardware startup that shares little with the pitch beyond a mobile-adjacent product and comparable failure stage."},{"candidate_id":"quibi::media_content","perspective_scores":{"business_model":{"score":4,"rationale":"Quibi was a mobile-first monthly subscription service, directly paralleling the subscription monetization model, though in media content rather than health."},"market":{"score":1,"rationale":"Quibi targeted general entertainment consumers, not chronic-illness patients, with no health or wellness demand driver overlap."},"gtm":{"score":4,"rationale":"Both used a direct-to-consumer mobile app subscription GTM, acquiring users through app stores and digital marketing."},"stage_scale":{"score":5,"rationale":"Quibi launched with massive funding and failed within 6 months at relatively early commercial stage, roughly comparable in time-to-failure though far better funded."}},"rationale":"Quibi shares the mobile-first monthly subscription model and direct digital GTM but operates in an entirely different sector with no overlap in target market or health value proposition."}]}
